section.title: The continuity equation
section.p:
gave the integral balance law:
\frac{d}{dt}\iiint_E \rho\,dV + \iint_{\partial E}\mathbf J\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS = \iiint_E s\,dV.
This equation counts a whole region. To turn it into a differential equation, we use the divergence theorem to move the boundary flux into the interior.

subsection.title: From a box balance to a local equation
subsection.p:
Return to the box E=[0,3]\times[0,1]\times[0,1] from . There we used
\rho(x,y,z,t)=10-t, \qquad \mathbf J(x,y,z,t)=(x,0,0).
There was no source: s=0.

The global balance A'(t)=-3 was not a coincidence. Inside every tiny box, the density decreases at rate 1, while the outward spreading of the flux contributes divergence 1. The two effects balance locally.

figure.caption: The local continuity equation. If more material leaves a tiny neighborhood than enters it (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} \gt 0), the local density must drop (\rho_t \lt 0) to balance the loss.

subsection.title: Deriving the local balance law
subsection.p:
Assume E is fixed. Then, as in single-variable calculus with a parameter, the time derivative can pass inside the integral:
\frac{d}{dt}\iiint_E \rho\,dV = \iiint_E \rho_t\,dV.
The divergence theorem gives
\iint_{\partial E}\mathbf J\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS = \iiint_E \nabla\cdot\mathbf J\,dV.
Substitute both into the integral balance law:
\iiint_E \rho_t\,dV + \iiint_E \nabla\cdot\mathbf J\,dV = \iiint_E s\,dV.
Combine the integrals:
\boxed{ \iiint_E \left( \rho_t+\nabla\cdot\mathbf J-s \right) dV = 0. }

proof.p: The proof is the calculation above, with one final observation.
Let g=\rho_t+\nabla\cdot\mathbf J-s. The integral balance law and the divergence theorem imply \iiint_E g\,dV=0 for every fixed solid region E.

If g(\mathbf p)\gt 0 at some point \mathbf p, then by continuity g is still positive in a small box around \mathbf p. The integral over that box would be positive, not zero. Similarly, g(\mathbf p)\lt 0 would give a small box with negative integral.
So g must be zero at every point: \rho_t+\nabla\cdot\mathbf J-s=0.

subsection.title: Why the hypotheses matter
subsection.p: Two small warnings prevent a common mistake.
First, an integral over one region being zero does not force the integrand to be zero.

For example, \int_{-1}^{1} x\,dx=0, but x is certainly not the zero function. The positive and negative parts cancel. That is why the theorem needs the balance law to hold for every small region, not merely for one large symmetric region.

Continuity rules out this kind of hidden spike. In more advanced courses, weak or distributional versions of conservation laws handle discontinuities such as shocks. In this book, the smooth case is the right first model.

subsection.title: The continuity equation for moving material
subsection.p: For a conserved quantity with no source, s=0, the local law is \boxed{\rho_t+\nabla\cdot\mathbf J=0.} If the material moves with velocity field \mathbf v, then \mathbf J=\rho\mathbf v. So the conservation law becomes \boxed{\rho_t+\nabla\cdot(\rho\mathbf v)=0.}
This is called the continuity equation.

statement.p: Let \rho(x,y,z,t) be a density transported by a velocity field \mathbf v(x,y,z,t). If the quantity is conserved and has no sources or sinks, then \boxed{\rho_t+\nabla\cdot(\rho\mathbf v)=0.} In coordinates, if \mathbf v=(v_1,v_2,v_3), then \boxed{\rho_t+(\rho v_1)_x+(\rho v_2)_y+(\rho v_3)_z=0.}

subsection.p: In the plane, the same equation is \boxed{\rho_t+(\rho v_1)_x+(\rho v_2)_y=0.}

subsection.title: A density profile moving down a pipe
subsection.p: In a long narrow pipe, suppose the velocity is constant: v=2. The continuity equation in one spatial dimension becomes \rho_t+(\rho v)_x=0, so \rho_t+(2\rho)_x=0. Since 2 is constant, \boxed{\rho_t+2\rho_x=0.}

Let f be any differentiable one-variable function, and define \rho(x,t)=f(x-2t). Then \rho_t=-2f'(x-2t), and \rho_x=f'(x-2t). Therefore
\rho_t+2\rho_x = -2f'(x-2t)+2f'(x-2t) = 0.
So every density profile of the form f(x-2t) solves the equation. The shape moves to the right at speed 2, without changing.

figure.caption: The solution \rho(x,t)=f(x-2t) moves to the right with speed 2.

subsection.p: A fixed sensor at one x-value may see the density rise and fall as the profile passes. But a tiny observer moving with speed 2 sees the same density value stay with it.

subsection.title: Expansion, compression, and the material derivative
subsection.p:
The compact equation ho_t+
abla\cdot(ho\mathbf v)=0 has another useful form. Expand the divergence using the product rule:

abla\cdot(ho\mathbf v) = \mathbf v\cdot
ablaho + ho\,
abla\cdot\mathbf v.
Thus  oxed{ho_t+\mathbf v\cdot
ablaho+ho\,
abla\cdot\mathbf v=0.}
So the continuity equation can be written as \boxed{\frac{D\rho}{Dt}+\rho\,\nabla\cdot\mathbf v=0.}

statement.p: In the plane, take \mathbf v(x,y)=(x,y). This field pushes points away from the origin. Its divergence is \nabla\cdot\mathbf v=1+1=2.

Suppose the density is uniform in space: \rho(x,y,t)=a(t). Then \nabla\rho=\mathbf 0, and the continuity equation becomes a'(t)+2a(t)=0. Hence \boxed{a(t)=Ce^{-2t}.} The material is conserved, but the flow expands area, so the density decreases.

Now take \mathbf v(x,y)=(-x,-y). Then \nabla\cdot\mathbf v=-1-1=-2. For a spatially uniform density \rho(x,y,t)=a(t), the continuity equation gives a'(t)-2a(t)=0. Therefore \boxed{a(t)=Ce^{2t}.} The flow compresses material toward the origin, so density rises.

subsection.p: Divergence is not just a symbolic derivative. It measures local expansion or compression of the flow.

subsection.title: Incompressible flow and divergence-free fields
subsection.p: A flow is called incompressible when tiny moving blobs keep the same volume in space, or the same area in the plane.

If \nabla\cdot\mathbf v=0, then the continuity equation becomes \boxed{\rho_t+\mathbf v\cdot\nabla\rho=0.} Equivalently, \boxed{\frac{D\rho}{Dt}=0.} The density is constant along the path of each moving particle.

statement.p:
The swirling velocity field \mathbf v(x,y)=(-y,x) has divergence
\nabla\cdot\mathbf v = \frac{\partial(-y)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial x}{\partial y} = 0+0 = 0.
So the flow is incompressible.
A small blob rotates around the origin. Its shape may turn, but to first order its area does not grow or shrink.

example.title: A shear flow with zero divergence

statement.p:
Let \mathbf v(x,y)=(y,0). Then
\nabla\cdot\mathbf v = \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial 0}{\partial y} = 0.
This flow is also incompressible.
Horizontal speed depends on height. A small square is sheared into a slanted parallelogram. Its shape changes, but its area does not.

figure.caption: Zero divergence does not mean no motion. It means no local area or volume change.

subsection.p: Divergence-free motion can still rotate, shear, fold, and stir. It simply does not create local expansion or compression.

subsection.title: The forms version of the continuity equation
subsection.p: The forms language shows the structure without extra words.

In space, the stored density is the 3-form \rho\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. The flux density \mathbf J=(J_1,J_2,J_3) has flux 2-form
\Phi_{\mathbf J} = J_1\,dy\wedge dz + J_2\,dz\wedge dx + J_3\,dx\wedge dy.
Its exterior derivative is
d\Phi_{\mathbf J} = (\nabla\cdot\mathbf J)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz.
The local balance law \rho_t+\nabla\cdot\mathbf J=s is therefore the form equation
\boxed{ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \bigl(\rho\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz\bigr) + d\Phi_{\mathbf J} = s\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. }

The continuity equation gives the shape of a conservation law, but it does not yet tell us what the flux \mathbf J should be. That choice comes from physics. Heat flows from hot to cold, so its flux is tied to the gradient of temperature. Waves carry tension and acceleration. Equilibrium removes time and leaves a condition with no sources. Different stories will now begin producing equations with the same skeleton: heat, wave, and Laplace equations.
